speaker: interviewer
text: Lily Feldman, F-E-L-D-M-A-N, scroll number two.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mrs. Feldman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to live with-- back with your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I went to this one room in this boarding house. It was run by a lady with her husband and her little girl, and I became friendly with her daughter because we were the same age and we went to the same school. By this time, I had gone to another school because I was living in another town. And I-- I couldn't wait, as I say, to get back to school to tell everybody that I had been bombed out and we didn't have anything. And to me, it was a new-- a very new experience living almost like a gypsy, because I had a very stable life. Everything was very stable. We had a very nice house with a front garden and a back garden, and-- and just very normal.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here I was in the middle of a war, having been bombed. Now I'm living in a one room with a kitchen, which was a cupboard. It really was a cupboard in-- down the hallway. My mother was absolutely devastated. And I was just living with them. We were living in one room. Oh, we had another little room off that, but it was for just eating in or whatever. I don't really remember. Did your father work during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father worked, and my father was also a firefighter. And he used to go-- he used to stay in the city of London two nights a week, and he used to be a fire watcher, what they called fire watchers. They would-- they would be civilian people who would help out by being in buildings and on top of buildings and walking around buildings in case incendiary bombs were dropped, which they were. And then they would alert-- they-- they had what they called these stirrup pump things, which were these pump things that you just stood on with water at the back. And you pumped and you tried to put them out, and sort of allay the-- the burning until the fire department came. And he did that on a volunteer basis, and so he was gone two nights a week. And it was all very, very exciting, until it apparently got to me. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got quite ill. I became quite ill. Oh, I have to go back and say this lady who had this-- she had these two large homes, these two large houses on this very nice street. One she had as a boarding house that she supplied food for the people. And the house where we lived in, she supp-- she had rooms with little kitchenettes or burners, and that's what we had. However, during the blitz, everybody would congregate-- all the tenants from the two houses would congregate in this lady's room in her house. And we would sing, and we would joke, and it was funny, and it was great. And we would listen to Vera Lynn on the radio, and we would listen to Alvar Lidell, who was the BBC news announcer, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] announcing all about the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they mentioned funny places in North Africa and we'd be hysterical listening to all these funny names that nobody had ever heard of. And oh, we've got to go. After the war, we're going to go back. We're going to go and see this place. Ever heard such a funny place in all your life as this place and that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a lot of fun. It was a lot, a lot of fun. We really enjoy-- and I enjoyed it. And of course, being up late, not having any sense of, well, now it's bedtime and now you have to do homework and now you have to do that. There was nothing structured, and I loved it. I really--

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did you have air raids?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every night. Every night, yes, while the blitz was going on, yes. And it really, even after we were bombed, didn't faze me. I wasn't-- I wasn't afraid of being bombed again. I really wasn't. I was with all these-- these people. We were having such a lot of fun. I really wasn't afraid at all. And when we were in school, we'd have to go under our desks or go to a basement. And then eventually, we didn't even do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was school ever disrupted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, yes. Sometimes it was, yes. And then, as soon as the air raids-- the all clear went, the teacher would say, well, I think now you can all go home. Or the headmistress, rather, would say, well, now you can all go home. But hurry, hurry. Go home as soon as you can. And another thing during the war which was very interesting-- everything was on coupons. Everything was rationed. Certain things weren't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother used to tell me, wherever I was, if you see a queue-- which is people standing in line-- if you see a queue, stand on it and buy whatever they're selling. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So sometimes they were selling tomatoes, because they were difficult to get. And sometimes they were selling oranges we couldn't get, and bananas we couldn't get. But certain things that were not actually rationed because there weren't enough to be rationed, you just took your chance and potluck. If you happened to be there when they had the supply of whatever it was, then you were-- you were lucky. And I remember one time, I was in some sort of a store. And there was a queue, so I stood on the end of it. What were they selling? Lipsticks. I bought a lipstick. Not for myself, for my mother. It was purple. And I mean, it was just the most outrageous color. My mother took one look at it and said, you wear this, you look like you got heart disease. But that's the way it was. And-- At that time, did you experience any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. At that time, no. No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not really, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you observe any holidays during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war? Good heavens, no. No, not at all. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- were you aware when the holidays came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really--

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I knew a little more when the Jewish refugees were in my school, but then I had more people to be with when the religious lessons were going on. I had company sitting outside. And most of the-- most of the girls were from Germany and Austria. And there was one little girl from Poland, who-- and at that time, I thought she was a very snobby, stuck-up little thing. And of course years later, I realize now, she really wasn't. She was just petrified. She changed-- her name was Helen Levy, and she would never use the name Levy. She insisted her name was Levee. And I think her name actually was Helenka, but she called herself Helen. And she would never say she was from Poland. She spoke very good English. Of course, her mother had a very thick, Polish-Jewish accent. But she-- I used to see her sometimes with her mother, and she always ignored people when she was with her mother. She was fairly friendly when she was in school, but she was a little bit strange. And I think now I-- I realize why.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other girls, the girls from Austria and Germany, I think most of them were from Germany, and even those from Germany said they were from Austria because it wasn't the right thing to do to say you were from Germany in those days. They were very-- what-- what's the word I want? Competent. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They really took everything in their stride very well, yes. I want to go back. You mentioned that you were in the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, yes. This was before the-- before the-- before the war, yes, yes. What about it?

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you had broken down, that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no, that was-- that's right. Oh, you mean during the war, when I-- yes. No, this was during the war. I became sick. Yes, I did. I had an earache-- started with an earache, and this was already 1941. We were bombed in 1940. November, 1940, we were bombed, and I became sick during the Easter holidays in 1941. And I got an earache, and the earache became very, very intense, so intense it was excruciating. And then my ears started to bleed. And my mother called the doctor, and the doctor came over. And I heard her whispering to my mother. And then the next thing I knew was that I was sent to the hospital. And my hand started to swell, and I got a very swollen hand. And I was put into a room, and I think I was delirious, because I remember being surrounded by lots of people in white. And I really don't remember too much. I just know that I was in an awful lot of pain. And I heard people saying, well, put a splint and do this and do that. And my-- they were fiddling with my ears. My ears were bleeding. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then my hand, it was getting bigger and bigger, and then they put my hand in a splint, and they were explaining to me, don't move your arm. It was so painful. And I-- I didn't know how I could not move it, I was in such pain. And then they realized, apparently, after I'd been there quite a few days-- they thought at first I had got rheumatic fever. And then they realized it wasn't rheumatic fever. The poison from my ear-- from one of my ears-- had traveled down to my-- down my arm, and it settled into my hand. And this was the reason for the swelling. And I had to have an emergency surgery to open up and have the poison drained, which is what they did. And I remember going into the operating room, and the doctor, who was-- one of the doctors was a Dr. Cohen. And he said, I'm Dr. Cohen, Lily. I'm Dr. Cohen, and don't worry. And I remember saying to him, oh, you're Jewish. Oh, now I know I'm going to be all right. I felt very comfortable with-- with him being Jewish. And so I recovered. It took a nice several months for me to recover, but I did recover. And then the doctor said to my mother, you cannot live-- you cannot bring your daughter back to this way of living. It's-- it's not right. It's-- it's too gypsy-like and it's not structured. You have to find a place to live, a proper place. She has to have her own room and you have to live a normal life. So I remember my mother going to the town hall, and they told her, well, find a place, and then we'll give you coupons for furniture. And-- and we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941 we found a place not too far from where we lived, and so I could go to the same school. And so we lived there. And we had all this terrible furniture, what they called utility furniture. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it was new, and I remember the mattress I had was one of those very lumpy things. You had to sort of shake it. It wasn't-- had no springs. It-- but it was a wartime mattress. And that's where we-- that's where we-- we lived until the-- and-- and I was perfectly content. It-- I was very happy. I had my own room and I was very happy there. Until the V-1s, the rockets started. And then I suppose I was getting older and I was getting more aware, and it wasn't fun anymore to be in the war. And it certainly-- I knew more about what was going on in Europe and how dreadful [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Germans were, the Nazis were, and I was just petrified that they would come into England. And the-- they had stopped all church bells in England. The only time a church bell would ring, they told people, was in case of an invasion. If the church bells rang, then you knew that they were sending the parachutists. The Germans were coming in. And I used to have nightmares. I would hear church bells all the time, and I would wake up absolutely petrified that they were coming in to get us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: 13. 12, 13, Yeah. Yeah. Yes, I was getting smarter, I suppose. And I was-- then I went to another school. I won a scholarship. I sat for it and I passed it to go to this Chiswick Polytechnic. And I was very happy about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there at the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there until 194-- 1944, because the-- the rockets started coming. And I became petrified of these rockets, and they were dreadful. At least with a plane, you heard the plane, and you heard it come over, and then you heard it disappear. With the rockets, it was entirely different. The V-1s, you heard this plane, and you knew it because it had a-- a very distinct sound, a chug, chug, chug. And then the-- the thing was, the engine would shut off. You'd hear this chug, chug, chug, chug, chug, and the engine would shut off. And you immediately, as soon as the engine shut off, you immediately made a dive for under the bed or under a table or somewhere safe. And then you heard an explosion, and that was how the V-1s were released. They-- they themselves were the bombs. And then the V-2s were a bigger-scale thing, and they just devastated whole streets. And I got very, very petrified and very nervous, and my mother took me away. My father couldn't leave, but my mother took me away to the north of England, to a place called Newcastle, where the coals come from, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to stay with her sister. And her sister was more Jewish. I mean, she practiced Judaism. So I got more into the swing of it, and I enjoyed it, and I liked it, and I went to synagogue. And we kept shabbos, and then I realized what I had been missing. And we stayed there until it was clear, and then we came back to London. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the war years, did your father hear from anyone from his family in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not directly. He-- especially his-- his youngest sister, he didn't hear from her at all. All the letters were returned with swastikas, and my father went white overnight. And she was such a lovely lady, she and her husband. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we found out-- I have to interject here about her-- we found out that they were one of the first who were sent to Poland, to Auschwitz. And they were given instructions-- be ready on this and this date, at this and this time, and this and this place, with one suitcase for shipment-- for-- for re-- what was it? What was the word they-- anyway, to be sent away to work. We need you for work in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we fo-- they-- they did it. They did it, and even though a lot of the neighbors said to them, well, you go, and you-- you and your husband go and leave the children with us, we'll take the children. And they said, no, where-- where we go, our children go. We wouldn't leave our children. We wouldn't go anywhere with-- without our children. But they gave some of their silverware and their [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] expensive stuff that they had, they did give to the neighbors, and the neighbors kept it until they returned. However, they never did return. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm very distressed, because she used-- they used to admonish the children when they were naughty. If you don't behave, you won't go with us to Poland, because the children were very excited, as children are when something new is going to happen. And they thought they were going on a trip to Poland. And they were admonished for being naughty that they wouldn't be allowed to go. And after the war, I did write to the Red Cross in Geneva. And what little information I had, I-- I gave to them, and they did answer me back that it was true that they were sent to Auschwitz and put into their records. That's where they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other sister was an invalid, and she had a very good doctor who gave her lots of letters for the Germans that she couldn't move, this and that. I don't know, whatever it was. And she was bedridden. And one night, they came for her. This truck pulled up outside her house. And who walked in, but her doctor and this German army officer. And he saw my aunt, this German. He wasn't an SS man. He was an army officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he walked in with his list. Apparently they had gone to this doctor to say, who are all your Jewish sick people? We don't want-- we don't want them. We want to get rid of them. And of course my aunt's name was on the list. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he wa-- he said, well, I have-- he made out with the Germans to go-- go with.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this army officer came in and saw my aunt lying in bed, and saw that she was indeed an invalid. And he got very emotional, and he said, I-- I'm a soldier. I fight soldiers. I don't fight bed-- bedridden women. And he crossed her name off the list, and he walked away. And she was never bothered again. But of course, a lot of her neighbors were. One neighbor, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they moved from one house to the next and just stayed there. And there's lots of stories of what-- how they survived. But that's how my family survived-- that particular aunt survived. But the other one, unfortunately, wasn't aware that-- that she was one-- that she and her husband were one of the first ones to be transported. Then later, people got smarter. They knew what to do. They went into hiding and friends hid them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know the war ended? You were living in northern England.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had come back. We knew the war was ending, and the rockets were no more, and it was safe to go back to London. So we weren't living there at the end of the war. We-- we came back to London when the war ended. And of course we were glued to the radio, and that's how we found out. We were just waiting. It was just always a matter of time. It was a matter of a few days, and finally, finally, it did end. And I remember I wanted to go up to Piccadilly to celebrate. My mother was really, very much against it. She argued, no, no, all those drunken people. You'll get [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pulled under, trodden, you know. People will tread on you, you'll fall, you'll this, you'll that. She was sort of like a hen with one chick, as far as I was concerned. And I remember having a big argument with her about that, that all the girls from school were going and why couldn't I go and this and that, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I did go, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after the war? What was your life like after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I-- we went back-- we went to Holland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had to get a permit. We had to get a visa, even though my father was a-- a Dutch national, as it were, but we had still. And then we stood in line for hours and hours and hours, and finally we managed. And we went to Holland and we saw the family. And it was sort of bittersweet, because one sister survived and the other one didn't. And then she had-- all the-- the friends had given this sister who survived all the bits and pieces from the other sister, including a beautiful, silver gallery tray that my mother had given her as a wedding gift. And they had said to my mother, well, this was your gift to her, so we're giving it back to you. My mother didn't want to know. She said, I don't want it, because it will remind me for the rest of my life. My mother became hysterical, and she wouldn't take it. She said, I just want to remember her as she was. I don't need any bits and pieces to remind me of her. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then I decided that I wasn't Jewish enough. I wanted to be more Jewish. There were too many Jews-- I found out how many Jews have been killed and what had happened to them, and this was absolutely devastating to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I told my parents, we are Jewish. I want to belong to a synagogue. And my father, he pooh-poohed the idea, and said, we don't need it. What do we need it for? It doesn't help us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, well, I want it. So when I said I want it, we did belong to-- we-- we did join a synagogue, and I started a youth group with other young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came back, yes, yes. Yes. And I had a very wonderful teenage life then, with all my Jewish friends and being very Jewish. And I loved it. And then I decided I wanted to go to Holland, and I did that.
